text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr H Patel
Westfield Health Centre

12 Westfield Road, London NW6 4AB
Dear Dr H Patel
James Walker, DOB: 14-02-1988, Hospital No: H937521 NHS No. 8421975309
21A Larkspur Close, London, SE15 3QN

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent seizures under ongoing review; aetiology and epilepsy classification not yet established. MRI brain (2023) reported no clear structural correlate. Routine EEG (2022) showed non-specific findings without definitive epileptiform discharges. No prior admission for status. Engages with a peer support group; peer observations may supplement history.

Previous medication: Sodium valproate (stopped due to tremor and weight gain), levetiracetam (mood irritability), carbamazepine (rash), lamotrigine (part-response), clobazam PRN (sedation), lacosamide (dizziness), perampanel (behavioural change), topiramate (word-finding difficulty), zonisamide (insomnia).

Not taken: brivaracetam, cenobamate, eslicarbazepine, pregabalin, tiagabine, rufinamide.

Comorbidities/other: Mild asthma; vitamin D insufficiency on supplementation. No history of head injury. Peer group facilitators note episodes witnessed during meetings with preserved ambulation and brief recovery, supporting caregiver descriptions.

I reviewed James Walker in clinic today, accompanied by his partner. He reported stable day-to-day functioning with structured routines and uses a seizure diary synchronised with feedback from his peer support group. He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 4 episodes weekly. These are occasionally accompanied by brief disorientation but resolve spontaneously. No generalised tonic-clonic seizures have occurred since last year. Caregivers feel overall control is acceptable. He denies recent injuries, tongue biting, or incontinence. Sleep remains fragmented, which he feels can precipitate events. He is not currently driving and understands DVLA guidance. Current medications listed below were reconciled with primary care and his community pharmacist.

Current medication:
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily
- Clobazam 5–10 mg PRN for anticipated clusters (rarely used)
- Salbutamol inhaler PRN
- Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily

Investigations/monitoring to date: Bloods in July 2025 within reference ranges (U&E, LFT, FBC). Lamotrigine level previously therapeutic (3.4 mg/L, May 2025). ECG normal sinus rhythm. Sleep diary suggests variability tied to late shifts.

Plan:
- Continue current anti-seizure regimen unchanged for now while we consolidate corroborative histories from caregivers and the peer support group; the patient will share their structured observation sheets.
- Arrange extended ambulatory EEG with event button to better capture habitual episodes, targeting within 8–10 weeks.
- Repeat serum lamotrigine level and general bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D) in 3 months or sooner if clinical change.
- Provide sleep hygiene advice; patient to trial consistent bedtime and limit night shifts where possible. He will record any change in frequency.
- Safety advice reinforced regarding bathing, cooking, heights, and public transport travel when fatigued.
- Community epilepsy nurse to check in by phone in 6 weeks to review diary and any adverse effects.
- Follow-up with me in clinic in 4 months, or earlier if there is an increase in seizure burden, injury, or adverse effects.

Thank you for your ongoing care and for prescribing repeats as above. I will update you following ambulatory EEG and once we have compiled the peer support group observations alongside the caregiver reports.

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'He has experienced ongoing focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically 4 episodes weekly.' This provides a clear current frequency. No other higher frequency is mentioned. No seizure-free period applies as he is having ongoing seizures.
